title: practical english usage - Part 10
content:
Examples: I'm; who've; John'll; can't.

Contractions are also made with non-auxiliary be and have.

Phonetic alphabet, page xxx). See also vowel.

consonant  for example, the letters b, c,d, f,g and their usual sounds (see used to join clauses together. Example: I rang because I was worried.

conjunction  a word like and, but, although, because, when, if, which  can be Supposing the train had been late, what would you have done?

Examples: If you try you'll understand; I would be surprised if she knew; conditional  a clause or sentence containing if (or a word with a similar meaning).

can experience by seeing, touching, etc. Examples: cloud; petrol; raspberry.

concrete noun (the opposite of an abstract noun)  the name of something which  we made of two or more parts. Examples: bus driver; get on with; one-eyed.

compound  a compound noun, verb, adjective, preposition, etc is one that is try phoning; down the street.

to complete its meaning. Examples: the intention to travel; full of water, 2. a structure or words needed after a noun, adjective, verb or preposition very kind; They elected him  President.

faster); also the structure more + adjective/adverb, used in the same way comparative  the form of an adjective or adverb made with -er (e.g. older, collective noun  a singular word for a group. Examples: family; team.

Language terminology •  xxii that something is more or less perfect, or very dead.

gradable words. Perfect or dead are not gradable words: we  do not usually say less pretty, hard or cold. Adverbs of degree (like rather, very) can be used with gradable  Pretty, hard or cold are gradable adjectives: things can be more or getting up early. See also present participle.

subject or object of a sentence). Examples: Smoking is bad for you; I hate gerund  the form of a verb ending in -ing, used like a noun (for example, as the Examples: he; she; it; who; which.

masculine, feminine and neuter, or between human and nonhuman.

gender  the use of different grammatical forms to show the difference between shall/will +be+ .. .ing. Example: I will be needing the car this evening.

future progressive (or future continuous)  a verb tense made with Example: I will have finished by lunchtime.

future perfect  a verb tense made with shall/will + have + past participle.

without to. Example: I will arrive on Tuesday evening.

future  a verb tense made with the auxiliary will (or sometimes shall) + infinitive full verb  a verb that is not an auxiliary verb. Examples: work, remove, explain.

emphasis. Example: Jack I like, but his wife I can't stand.

fronting  moving a part of a clause to the beginning in order to give it special often; never, daily; occasionally; every three days.

frequency  Adverbials of frequency say how  often something happens. Examples: more formal word than start.

in some literary writing, in business letters, etc. For example, commence is a formal  the style used when talking politely to strangers, on special occasions, first person  see person.

grammars; other forms (e.g written, playing) are called 'non-fmite'.

finite verb  Verbs which  show time (e.g. goes, went) are often called ‘ finite’  in ending  something added to the end of a word, e.g. -er, -ing, -ed.

president himself. See also reflexive pronoun.

a noun or pronoun. Examples: I'll tell him  myself, I wouldn't sell this to the emphatic pronoun  reflexive pronoun (myself yourself, etc) used to emphasise by using do in an affirmative clause; by using special word order).
